vulnerability.title: Disable SSH Support for User Known Hosts
vulnerability.description: SSH can allow system users to connect to systems if a cache of the remote systems public keys is available. This should be disabled. To ensure this behavior is disabled, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: IgnoreUserKnownHosts yes
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreUserKnownHosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreUserKnownHosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreUserKnownHosts\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" cp "/etc/s

triage_decision.reason: This is a standard SSH hardening measure that disables the use of user-known hosts for authentication, reducing the risk of spoofing. It is a simple configuration change with low risk of system breakage when applied correctly.

commands_executed: for f in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf; do [ -f "$f" ] && sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreUserKnownHosts\s\+/Id" "$f"; done

per_turn.command: for f in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf; do [ -f "$f" ] && sed -i "/^\s*IgnoreUserKnownHosts\s\+/Id" "$f"; done

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
The user wants me to remediate an OpenSCAP finding related to SSH configuration. The finding is about disabling SSH support for user known hosts. The rule is "sshd_disable_user_known_hosts" with rule ID xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sshd_disable_user_known_hosts.

According to the remediation recommendation, I need to:
1. Ensure the file /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf exists
2. Set the value of IgnoreUserKnownHosts to "yes" in that file
3. Remove any existing IgnoreUserKnownHosts lines from /etc/ssh/sshd_config and any other files in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/ to avoid conflicts

The plan should be:
1. First, check if /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf exists
2. If it doesn't exist, create it with proper permissions (0600)
3. Remove any existing IgnoreUserKnownHosts lines from /etc/ssh/sshd_config and all files in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/
4. Add the line "IgnoreUserKnownHosts yes" to /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf

I need to follow the workflow:
1. PLAN: Describe my proposed fix
2. REVIEW: Call review_plan with my full plan description BEFORE executing any commands
3. APPLY: Only after review_plan returns approved=true, use run_cmd / read_file / write_file to apply the fix

Let me first create a detailed plan description for review.

4. Remove any existing IgnoreUserKnownHosts lines from all files in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/

6. Reload SSH service to apply changes

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan would resolve the vulnerability by ensuring the required configuration is in place. However, it lacks some robustness checks and best practices that would make it more reliable and maintainable.
concerns: Does not verify the platform condition (rpm --quiet -q kernel-core) before applying, which may cause issues on unsupported systems
Does not ensure a newline at the end of the configuration file, which can cause parsing issues
Does not handle the case where the file exists but already contains the correct directive (unnecessary modifications)
Does not reload or restart the SSH service after configuration changes, which is typically required for changes to take effect
suggested_improvements: Add the platform check (rpm --quiet -q kernel-core) to ensure remediation is only applied on supported systems
Ensure the configuration file ends with a newline using sed -i -e '$a\' before appending the directive
Check if the directive already exists with the correct value before modifying to avoid unnecessary changes
Include a service reload/restart step (e.g., systemctl reload sshd) to apply the configuration
Use more robust file existence checks and error handling for production reliability
